q: HI Ad Click Aggregator: how do you make click tracking idempotent (dedupe retries)?
options: Use the userId as the dedup key
Generate a unique impression ID per ad impression and dedupe on it
Count only the first click per minute
why: HI: mint a unique impression ID when the ad is served and carry it on the click event, so retried/duplicate clicks collapse to one. userId is too coarse.

q: HI Web Crawler: which queue does HI pick for the frontier, and what goes in it?
options: SQS, storing only the URL (visibility timeout gives easy backoff + DLQ)
A SQL table of HTML blobs
why: HI picks SQS: its visibility timeout makes retries/exponential backoff and dead-letter queues easy. Store a URL pointer, not raw HTML (HTML goes to S3) — keep the message small and the URL in-flight until the HTML is confirmed stored.

q: HI Web Crawler: a surprising bottleneck when crawling billions of pages is…
options: DNS resolution (cited as up to ~70% of per-thread time) — fix with DNS caching and multiple providers
why: HI (citing the Mercator paper): DNS can dominate thread time. Cache DNS results and round-robin multiple DNS providers. Crawling is I/O-bound, so a handful of network-optimized machines suffices for the 5-day target.

q: HI Web Crawler: which are part of being a 'polite' crawler? (select all)
options: Rate-limit ~1 request/sec per domain (per-domain Redis SET NX lock)
Add jitter to avoid synchronized retries
why: HI: respect robots.txt including Crawl-delay, throttle per domain (~1 rps via a per-domain lock), and add jitter. Ignoring robots.txt is the opposite of polite.

q: HI Web Crawler: how do you avoid re-storing the same content and getting stuck in crawler traps?
options: Crawl each URL only once ever
Dedupe by content hash (not just URL) and cap crawl depth (~15–20 hops from seed)
why: HI: URL-level dedup misses mirror content, so also hash page content. Cap depth (hops from the seed, not path segments) to escape infinite trap pages.

q: HI Top-K: the headline data structure when exact counts can be relaxed to approximate is…
options: A Count-Min Sketch (plus a heap for the top-K)
why: HI: a Count-Min Sketch estimates per-item counts in megabytes instead of a hundreds-of-GB exact hash table; feed estimates into a heap to keep the top-K. Only valid when 'precise' is relaxed.

q: HI Top-K: 70B views/day (~700k TPS) far exceeds one DB. How do you keep results exact?
options: Ingest via Kafka, shard the counts across many DBs, compute each shard's top-K and merge
Round counts to the nearest thousand
why: HI: buffer with Kafka and shard counters; each shard's local top-K, merged, is mathematically guaranteed to contain the global top-K — exact, without a single hot DB.

q: HI Top-K: why does HI restrict the problem to tumbling (non-overlapping) windows?
options: Tumbling windows are less accurate, which is fine
Sliding windows are substantially harder to maintain; tumbling keeps aggregation tractable
Users only ask for sliding windows
why: HI: sliding windows require continuously re-aggregating overlapping ranges; tumbling windows let you keep simple per-window aggregates (precomputed/cached per minute).

q: HI Rate Limiter: why does a per-gateway in-memory counter fail?
options: The load balancer spreads a client's requests across gateways, so each sees only a fraction of the count — use a centralized Redis
It only works for IP-based limits
why: HI: with traffic spread across gateway instances, no single instance has the true count. Centralize the counter in Redis (sharded by client key) so the limit is enforced globally.

q: HI Rate Limiter: how do you make the read-modify-write of a token bucket race-free in Redis?
options: Run the check/refill/decrement as one atomic unit (MULTI/EXEC or a Lua script), plus EXPIRE to avoid leaks
why: HI: bundle the HGET tokens / refill / HSET into an atomic transaction (or Lua) so concurrent requests can't both spend the last token; set EXPIRE so idle buckets don't leak memory.

q: HI Rate Limiter: if the Redis backing the limiter goes down, does HI fail open or fail closed (for a social API)?
options: Fail open — never block legitimate users
Fail closed — reject requests, because outages often coincide with spikes and failing open can collapse the backend
why: HI calls both defensible but picks fail-closed for social media: limiter failures tend to coincide with traffic surges, so failing open could let a flood cascade into a backend collapse.

q: HI Google Docs: which concurrency-control approach does HI choose, and what's the alternative?
options: Locks per paragraph; alternative is no locking
Operational Transformation (OT, what Google uses); CRDTs are the alternative for P2P/larger collaborator counts (e.g. Figma)
why: HI: OT transforms concurrent INSERT/DELETE ops against each other on a central server so all clients converge — low memory, great for text. CRDTs suit peer-to-peer or very large collaboration but cost more memory.

q: HI Google Docs: what should clients send over the WebSocket?
options: The entire document on every keystroke
Edits as operations (e.g. INSERT/DELETE at a position)
Only the final document on save
why: HI: transmit compact operations (insert/delete at offsets), not the whole doc — that's what OT transforms and what Cassandra stores append-only, partitioned by documentId.

q: HI Google Docs: how do you ensure all editors of one document hit the same WebSocket server at scale?
options: Consistent hashing by documentId, with ZooKeeper maintaining the hash ring; client connects via HTTP then upgrades to WS on the right server
why: HI: route by documentId via consistent hashing so collaborators converge on one stateful server; ZooKeeper keeps the ring consistent as servers join/leave. Snapshot/compact operations to bound storage/transfer.

q: HI Payment System: a request times out after charging. How do you avoid double-charging on retry?
options: Assume the timeout means it failed and retry blindly
Idempotency keys, plus reconciliation against the payment network — never assume a timeout equals failure
why: HI: a timeout is ambiguous (it may have succeeded). An idempotency key makes the retry a no-op if it already went through, and reconciliation against the network catches mismatches.

q: HI Payment System: what storage approach gives the durability/audit trail payments require?
options: Store only current state in a single table
Event sourcing: an append-only immutable event log (via CDC), not just current state
why: HI: append-only event log is the foundation for audits, chargebacks, reconciliation and fraud detection — current-state-only loses the history regulators and disputes require.

q: HI Payment System: how is raw card data handled for PCI compliance?
options: Encrypt it in your primary DB
Tokenization — the card is exchanged for a token so your backend never stores/handles the raw card number
why: HI: tokenize card data (e.g. via the provider's client-side SDK) so raw card numbers never reach your servers, keeping you out of PCI scope. Pair with API keys + request signing for security.

q: For a typical read-heavy product (social app, marketplace), which components belong in the baseline high-level design? (select all)
options: API gateway / load balancer as the entry point
A primary database for core entities
A cache (e.g. Redis) in front of hot reads
A CDN for static assets (images, JS, video)
A graph database — always, for any app
why: The standard product-design skeleton is: clients → API gateway/LB → stateless services → primary DB, with a cache for hot reads and a CDN for static media.

q: What is HI's delivery-framework order for driving a design?
options: Jump straight to the database schema
Requirements (functional + non-functional) → core entities → API → high-level design → deep dives
Draw the whole architecture first, then justify it
why: HI's framework: nail requirements (functional + non-functional), define core entities, sketch the API, build the high-level design, then go deep on the hard parts and trade-offs.

q: Which non-functional requirements should you pin down early in almost any design? (select all)
options: Which programming language the team uses
why: Non-functional requirements (the '-ilities') shape every later decision: availability-vs-consistency, latency, throughput/scale, and durability.

q: Which cross-cutting concerns should a senior+ candidate proactively address in any distributed design? (select all)
options: Fault tolerance / eliminating single points of failure
why: Beyond the happy path, name observability, fault tolerance/SPOF removal, security, and how each tier scales horizontally — these are the operational signals interviewers look for at senior+.

q: A requirement says 'users worldwide need fast loading of images and video'. Which component directly addresses it?
options: A CDN caching static assets at edge locations
why: A CDN caches static content at edge locations close to users, cutting latency and offloading the origin — the standard answer for global static delivery.

q: A requirement says 'video encoding takes minutes and must not block the upload request'. Which pattern fits?
options: Do it synchronously in the request
Ack immediately + push a job to a queue; a worker pool processes it in the background
why: This is the Managing Long-Running Tasks pattern: validate + enqueue + return a job ID in milliseconds, while separate workers do the heavy processing.

q: You need full-text search over millions of posts. Which component, and why not just the primary DB?
options: Add Elasticsearch — an inverted index makes full-text search fast; LIKE forces a full table scan
why: Full-text search needs an inverted index (term → documents). Elasticsearch is the go-to; a leading-wildcard LIKE can't use a B-tree index and scans the whole table.

q: Which subsystems are essential for a URL shortener? (select all)
options: A write/create service that generates the short code
A key-value store mapping short code → long URL
why: Bitly decomposes into a write path (generate unique code, store mapping), a read/redirect path, a KV store for the mapping, and a cache because reads vastly outnumber writes.

q: Which subsystems are essential for a file storage/sync service like Dropbox? (select all)
options: A presigned-URL service for direct client upload/download
A sync/notification service to propagate changes
A full-text search cluster over file contents
why: Core pieces: blob storage for bytes, a metadata DB for file/version/share info, presigned URLs so clients transfer directly to S3, and a sync/notification path to keep devices consistent.

q: Which subsystems are essential for Ticketmaster? (select all)
options: An event + search service (Elasticsearch) for browsing/finding events
A booking service backed by an ACID database (no double-booking)
A seat-reservation mechanism (distributed lock with TTL)
A real-time ML recommendation training pipeline
why: Ticketmaster needs search (ES), a transactional booking service (ACID prevents double-booking), a reservation hold (Redis lock w/ TTL), and payment integration.

q: Which subsystems are essential for a social news feed? (select all)
options: A follow/social-graph store (table + GSI for reverse lookups)
A feed-generation path (fan-out workers / precomputed feed)
A feed cache for hot reads
why: Feed systems need posts, the follow graph (with a reverse index), feed generation (fan-out on write/read or hybrid), and a feed cache.

q: Which subsystems are essential for a chat app like WhatsApp? (select all)
options: A pub/sub routing layer to reach the right server (e.g. Redis Pub/Sub per user)
An inbox/message store for offline delivery
An OLAP data warehouse for the core chat path
why: Chat needs persistent WebSocket servers, a routing layer so a message reaches whichever server holds the recipient's connection, durable inboxes for offline users, and presence.

q: Which subsystems are essential for a ride-sharing service like Uber? (select all)
options: A location service with a geospatial index (Redis geohashing)
A ride-matching service (with a lock to avoid double-dispatch)
why: Uber needs high-frequency location ingestion + proximity search (geospatial index), matching (with a lock so one driver isn't double-assigned), maps/fare, and notifications.

q: Which subsystems are essential for a near-real-time ad click aggregator? (select all)
options: A stream processor for windowed aggregation (Flink)
An OLAP store for advertiser analytics queries
A data lake (S3) + batch (Spark) for reconciliation
A transactional RDBMS as the primary aggregation store
why: The pipeline is stream ingestion → Flink aggregation → OLAP for queries, plus a Lambda-architecture batch layer (S3 + Spark) to reconcile and serve as source of truth.

q: Which subsystems are essential for a large-scale web crawler? (select all)
options: A frontier queue of URLs to fetch (SQS)
Blob storage (S3) for raw HTML / extracted text
A metadata DB for dedup and crawl state
why: A crawler needs a frontier queue, fetch + extract workers, blob storage for content, a metadata DB (URL/content dedup + state), and DNS resolution (a real bottleneck).

q: Which subsystems are essential for a collaborative document editor? (select all)
options: Coordination for routing editors of one doc to one server (ZooKeeper-maintained hash ring)
A CDN to serve the live document
why: Real-time editing needs WebSocket servers, an OT engine to converge concurrent edits, an append-only ops store, and coordination (consistent hashing by docId via ZooKeeper) so collaborators share a server.

q: Which subsystems are essential for a payment system like Stripe? (select all)
options: A transaction service that talks to payment networks
An append-only event log (event sourcing) for audit + reconciliation
why: Payments need a secure gateway, a payment-intent service + DB, a transaction service to external networks, and an immutable event log enabling audit, reconciliation, and chargebacks.

q: Requirement: 'prevent abuse / DDoS before traffic reaches our services'. Which component and where?
options: A cache in front of the DB
A rate limiter at the edge (API gateway), keyed per user/IP/API key
why: Put a rate limiter at the edge (API gateway/LB) so abusive traffic is rejected before it loads downstream services; key it per user/IP/API key for fairness.

q: Requirement: 'hold a limited resource (seat, inventory) for a few minutes during checkout'. Which mechanism?
options: A distributed lock with a TTL (e.g. Redis), auto-releasing on abandon
why: A distributed lock with a TTL holds the resource for a bounded time and auto-releases if the user abandons — the standard reservation pattern (Ticketmaster, Uber dispatch).

q: Requirement: 'low-latency two-way realtime messaging'. Which transport?
why: WebSocket gives full-duplex, low-latency bidirectional messaging over one persistent connection — right for chat/collaboration.

q: Requirement: 'store user-uploaded videos and serve them globally'. Which storage layout?
options: Store video bytes as BLOBs in the primary DB
Store videos in blob storage (S3) + a CDN, keep only the URL/reference in the DB
why: Large binaries go in blob storage (S3) fronted by a CDN; the DB stores just the URL/reference. Binaries in the DB wreck index/cache efficiency and cost far more.

q: Requirement: 'advertisers run analytical queries (sums/group-bys) over billions of events with sub-second latency'. Which store?
options: The transactional row store that ingests the events
An OLAP / columnar store (ClickHouse, Druid, BigQuery)
why: Analytical aggregation over huge event volumes belongs in an OLAP/columnar store optimized for scans and group-bys — separate from the transactional ingest path.

q: Requirement: 'three independent teams must each process the same event stream their own way'. Which technology/feature?
options: SQS, where the message is deleted after one consumer acks
Kafka with a separate consumer group per team (each tracks its own offset)
A single shared database table they poll and delete from
why: Kafka retains the log and lets multiple consumer groups read it independently, each with its own offset — exactly the multi-consumer case.

q: Requirement: 'find drivers/restaurants within N km, fast, over millions of entities'. Which component?
options: A geospatial index (Redis geo, PostGIS, or ES geo-queries)
why: Proximity search needs a geospatial index (geohash/quadtree-based) — a B-tree can't efficiently answer 2D radius queries.

q: Requirement: 'coordinate which server is the leader / keep a consistent shared config across a cluster'. Which component?
options: A coordination service like ZooKeeper (leader election, config, failure detection)
why: Distributed coordination — leader election, consistent configuration, failure detection — is ZooKeeper's job, using a hierarchical znode namespace + watches with strong consistency (ZAB).
